questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2024
questions.question: A particle of mass $m$ and charge $q$ describes a circular path of radius $R$ in a magnetic field. If its mass and charge were $2 m$ and $\frac{q}{2}$ respectively, the radius of its path would be
questions.solution:
To determine the radius of the path when the mass and charge of the particle change, we first recognize that the magnetic force acting on a charged particle moving in a magnetic field is equal to the centripetal force, allowing the particle to move in a circular path.

The relationship is described as follows:

$ \text{Magnetic force} = \text{Centripetal force} $

Given by the equation:

$ qvB = \frac{mv^2}{R} $

Rearranging the terms, we can express the radius $ R $ as:

$ R = \frac{mv}{qB} $

Now, considering the new conditions, where the mass $ m $ changes to $ 2m $ and the charge $ q $ changes to $ \frac{q}{2} $:

Substitute $ m = 2m $ and $ q' = \frac{q}{2} $:

$ \frac{q}{2} B = \frac{2mv}{R'} $

    Solving for the new radius $ R' $:

    $ R' = \frac{4mv}{qB} $

    This shows that:

    $ R' = 4R $

    Therefore, under the new conditions where the mass is doubled and the charge is halved, the radius of the path becomes four times the original radius $ R $.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2024
questions.question:
Assertion (A): The energy of a charged particle moving in a magnetic field does not change.

Reason (R): It is because the work done by the magnetic force on the charge moving in a magnetic field is zero.
options:
If both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is correct explanation of Assertion (A).
If both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
If Assertion (A) is true but Reason (R) is false.
If both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are false.
questions.solution: As the velocity and applied magnetic field are perpendicular to each other, hence, the work done by a magnetic force on a charge moving in a magnetic field is zero. This also means that the energy of the particle will not change. So, both the assertion and reason are true and reason correctly explains the assertion.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2024
questions.question: Derive an expression for magnetic force $\vec{F}$ acting on a straight conductor of length $L$ carrying current I in an external magnetic field $\vec{B}$. Is it valid when the conductor is in zig-zag form? Justify.
questions.solution:
Since, the force on an individual charge moving at the drift velocity $v_d$

$$F=q v_d B \sin \theta$$

For N charge carriers

$$\begin{aligned} F & =\left(q v_d B \sin \theta\right)(N) \\ \text{where,}\quad N & =n V=n A L \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad F & =q v_d B \sin \theta n A L \\ F & =n q A v_d B \sin \theta L \\ F & =I L B \sin \theta \quad\left[\because I=n q A v_d\right] \\ \vec{F} & =I(\vec{L} \times \vec{B}) \end{aligned}$$

Yes, it is valid even when the conductor is in zig-zag form as then also the length of the wire will remain at the same angle with the magnetic field as before.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2024
questions.question: An electron moving with a velocity $\vec{v}=\left(1.0 \times 10^7\right.$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}) \hat{i}+\left(0.5 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right) \hat{j}$ enters a region of uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}=(0.5 \mathrm{mT}) \hat{j}$. Find the radius of the circular path described by it. While rotating; does the electron trace a linear path too? If so, calculate the linear distance covered by it during the period of one revolution.
questions.solution:
Given, $\quad \begin{aligned} \vec{v} & =\left(1.0 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right) \hat{i}+\left(0.5 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right) \hat{j} \\ \vec{B} & =(0.5 \mathrm{mT}) \hat{j}\end{aligned}$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Since, } \quad \frac{m v^2}{r}=q v B\\ &\Rightarrow \quad r=\frac{m v_x}{q B}=\frac{\left(9.10 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.0 \times 10^7\right)}{\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\right)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} r & =11.3875 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m} \\ r & =11.39 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$$

Now,

$$\begin{aligned} & T=\frac{2 \pi r}{v}=\frac{2 \pi m}{q B} \\ & T=\frac{2 \times 3.14 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}} \\ & T=71.51 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~s} \\ & T=71.51 \mathrm{~ns} \end{aligned}$$

The electron traces a linear path too. For one revolution pitch,

$$\begin{aligned} T \times v_y & =71.51 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.5 \times 10^7 \\ & =35.75 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m} \\ & =35.75 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2024 Paper 1 Online
questions.question:
An infinitely long wire, located on the $z$-axis, carries a current $I$ along the $+z$-direction and produces the magnetic field $\vec{B}$. The magnitude of the line integral $\int \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}$ along a straight line from the point $(-\sqrt{3} a, a, 0)$ to $(a, a, 0)$ is given by

[ $\mu_0$ is the magnetic permeability of free space.]
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d} \ell}|=\mathrm{rd} \theta \\ & \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}|=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}} \\ & \Rightarrow \int \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d} \ell}=\int|\overline{\mathrm{B}}||\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d} \ell}| \cos 0^{\circ} \\ & =\int\left(\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}\right) \times(\mathrm{rd} \theta) \\ & =\int_\limits{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} \frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{2 \pi} \mathrm{d} \theta=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{2 \pi}\left[\theta_2-\left(-\theta_1\right)\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\text { [ } \theta_1 \text { is anticlockwise hence taken negative] }$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \tan \theta_1=\frac{a \sqrt{3}}{a}=\sqrt{3} \\ & \theta_1=\frac{\pi}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \theta_2=\frac{a}{a}=1 \\ & \theta_2=\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \int B \cdot d \ell=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\pi}{4}\right] \\ & =\frac{7 \mu_0 I}{24} \\ & \Rightarrow \text { Ans. Option (A) } \end{aligned}$$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2024 Paper 2 Online
questions.question: A thin stiff insulated metal wire is bent into a circular loop with its two ends extending tangentially from the same point of the loop. The wire loop has mass $m$ and radius $r$ and it is in a uniform vertical magnetic field $B_0$, as shown in the figure. Initially, it hangs vertically downwards, because of acceleration due to gravity $g$, on two conducting supports at $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{Q}$. When a current $I$ is passed through the loop, the loop turns about the line $\mathrm{PQ}$ by an angle $\theta$ given by
questions.solution:
Let loop makes angle $$\theta$$ with vertical.

in equilibrium $$\tau_{\text {net }}=0$$

$$\tau_0=\mathrm{MB} \sin (90-\theta)-\mathrm{mg} . \mathrm{r} \sin \theta=0$$

$$\mathrm{I}$$. $$\pi \mathrm{r}^2 \cdot \mathrm{B}_0 \cos \theta=\mathrm{mg} \mathrm{r} \cdot \sin \theta$$

$$\tan \theta=\frac{\pi \mathrm{rIB}_0}{\mathrm{mg}}$$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2024 Paper 2 Online
questions.question:
A positive, singly ionized atom of mass number $A_{\mathrm{M}}$ is accelerated from rest by the voltage $192 \mathrm{~V}$. Thereafter, it enters a rectangular region of width $w$ with magnetic field $\vec{B}_0=0.1 \hat{k}$ Tesla, as shown in the figure. The ion finally hits a detector at the distance $x$ below its starting trajectory.

[Given: Mass of neutron/proton $=(5 / 3) \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$, charge of the electron $=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$.]

Which of the following option(s) is(are) correct?
options: The value of $x$ for an ion with $A_{\mathrm{M}}=144$ is $48 \mathrm{~cm}$.
For detecting ions with $1 \leq A_{\mathrm{M}} \leq 196$, the minimum height $\left(x_1-x_0\right)$ of the detector is $55 \mathrm{~cm}$.
The minimum width $w$ of the region of the magnetic field for detecting ions with $A_{\mathrm{M}}=196$ is $56 \mathrm{~cm}$.
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} & x=2 R \\ & \Rightarrow x=2 \frac{P}{q B} \Rightarrow x=\frac{2 \sqrt{2 m q V}}{q B} \Rightarrow x=\frac{2}{B} \sqrt{\frac{2 m V}{q}} \end{aligned}$$

Option A

For $$\mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{m}=\frac{5}{3} \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$$

$$\therefore x=\frac{2}{0.1} \sqrt{\frac{2 \times \frac{5}{3} \times 10^{-27} \times 192}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$$

Option B

For $$\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{m}}=144$$

$$x=\frac{2}{0.1} \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 144 \times \frac{5}{3} \times 10^{-27} \times 192}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}}=48 \mathrm{~cm}$$

Option C

for $$\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{m}}=1$$

$$\mathrm{x}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$$ & for $$\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{m}}=196$$

$$\mathrm{x}=56 \mathrm{~cm}$$.

so $$\mathrm{x}_0=4 \mathrm{~cm}$$ & $$\mathrm{x}_1=56 \mathrm{~cm}$$

$$\therefore \mathrm{x}_1-\mathrm{x}_0=52 \mathrm{~cm}$$.

Option D

Minimum width $$=\mathrm{R}$$

for $$\mathrm{A}_M=196$$

$$\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{qB}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mqV}}}{\mathrm{qB}}$$

$$\mathrm{R}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~B}} \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{mV}}{\mathrm{q}}}$$

$$\mathrm{w}_{\min }=\mathrm{R}=\frac{1}{0.1} \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 196 \times \frac{5}{3} \times 10^{-27} \times 192}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}}=28 \mathrm{~cm}$$

questions.title: BITSAT 2024
questions.question: The Boolean expression $ \sim(p \vee q) \vee(\sim p \wedge q) $ is equivalent to
questions.solution:
The Boolean expression $ \sim(p \vee q) \vee (\sim p \wedge q) $ is simplified as follows:

Start with the given expression:

$ \sim(p \vee q) \vee (\sim p \wedge q) $

Apply De Morgan's Law:

$ (\sim p \wedge \sim q) \vee (\sim p \wedge q) $

Use the Distributive Law:

$ \sim p \wedge (\sim q \vee q) $

By the Complement Law, $(\sim q \vee q)$ is a tautology ($t$):

$ \sim p \wedge t $

Finally, apply the Identity Law:

$ \sim p $

Therefore, the expression simplifies to $ \sim p $.

questions.title: BITSAT 2024
questions.question: If $ A=\frac{1}{3}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & -2 \\ a & 2 & b\end{array}\right] $ is an orthogonal matrix, then
questions.solution:
We know that, if $ A $ is an orthogonal matrix, then

$ A A^{T}=I $

$ \begin{array}{c} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{3}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & -2 \\ a & 2 & b \end{array}\right] \cdot \frac{1}{3}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & a \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & -2 & b \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & -2 \\ a & 2 & b \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & a \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & -2 & b \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 9 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 9 \end{array}\right] \\ \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 9 & 0 & a+4+2 b \\ 0 & 9 & 2 a+2-2 b \\ a+2 b & 2 a+2-2 b & a^{2}+4+b^{2} \end{array}\right] \\ =\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 9 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 9 \end{array}\right] \end{array} $

$ \Rightarrow \quad a+4+2 b=0 \text { and } 2 a+2-2 b=0 $

$ \Rightarrow \quad a+2 b=-4 \quad \ldots \text { (i) } $

and $ a-b=-1 \quad \ldots \text { (ii) } $

By solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$ a=-2, b=-1 $

questions.title: BITSAT 2024
questions.question: If matrix $ A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}3 & -2 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right] $ and $ A^{-1}=\frac{1}{k} \operatorname{adj}(A) $,
questions.solution:
Given the matrix $ A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -2 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $ and $ A^{-1} = \frac{1}{k} \operatorname{adj}(A) $, we want to determine the value of $ k $.

We know from the properties of matrices that:

$ A^{-1} = \frac{\operatorname{adj}(A)}{|A|} $

By comparing this equation with the given relationship $ A^{-1} = \frac{1}{k} \operatorname{adj}(A) $, we can identify that:

$ k = |A| $

Therefore, we need to calculate the determinant $|A|$ of the matrix $ A $:

$ |A| = \left| \begin{array}{ccc} 3 & -2 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{array} \right| $

To find $|A|$, expand across the first row:

$ |A| = 3 \begin{vmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{vmatrix} - (-2) \begin{vmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix} + 4 \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix} $

Calculate each of the 2x2 determinants:

$ \begin{vmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = (2 \times 1) - (-1 \times 1) = 2 + 1 = 3 $

$ \begin{vmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = (1 \times 1) - (0 \times -1) = 1 $

$ \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = (1 \times 1) - (0 \times 2) = 1 $

Substitute these values back into the determinant calculation:

$ |A| = 3 \times 3 + 2 \times 1 + 4 \times 1 = 9 + 2 + 4 = 15 $

Thus, the value of $ k $ is $|A| = 15$.

questions.title: BITSAT 2024
questions.question: Suppose $ p, q, r \neq 0 $ and system of equation $ (p+a) x+b y+c z=0 $, $ a x+(q+b) y+c z=0 $, $ a x+b y+(r+c) z=0 $, has a non-trivial solution, then the value of $ \frac{a}{p}+\frac{b}{q}+\frac{c}{r} $ is
questions.solution:
Let $ |A|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}p+a & b & c \\ a & q+b & c \\ a & b & r+c\end{array}\right| $

Also, given that equations has non trivial solution $ \therefore $

$ |A|=0 $

$ \begin{aligned} & \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} p+a & b & c \\ a & q+b & c \\ a & b & r+c \end{array}\right|=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (p+a)[(q+b)(r+c)-b c]-b[a(r+c)-c a] +c[a b-a(q+b)]=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (p+a)[q r+q c+b r]-b[a r]+c[-a q]=0 \\ \Rightarrow & p q r+p q c+p b r+a q r=0 \end{aligned} $

Dividing whole equation by $ p q r $

$ \begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \frac{p q r}{p q r}+\frac{p q c}{p q r}+\frac{p r b}{p q r}+\frac{q r a}{p q r}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 1+\frac{c}{r}+\frac{b}{q}+\frac{a}{p}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{a}{p}+\frac{b}{q}+\frac{c}{r}=-1 \end{array} $

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2024 Paper 1 Online
questions.solution:
$$\alpha, \beta$$ are roots of $$x^2+x-1=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \alpha+\beta=-1 \Rightarrow 1+\alpha+\beta=0 \\ & M=\left[\begin{array}{lll} \mathrm{a}_{11} & \mathrm{a}_{12} & \mathrm{a}_{13} \\ \mathrm{a}_{21} & \mathrm{a}_{22} & \mathrm{a}_{23} \\ \mathrm{a}_{31} & \mathrm{a}_{32} & \mathrm{a}_{33} \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

(P) $$\quad M=\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & \alpha & \beta \\ \alpha & \beta & 1 \\ \beta & 1 & \alpha\end{array}\right] \Rightarrow 3 ! \times 2=12$$

For one arrangement of row 1 we can arrange other two rows exactly in two ways and row 1 can be arranged in 3! ways

$$\therefore 3!\times 2=12 \text { ways }$$

(Q) $$\quad M=\left[\begin{array}{lll}x & a & b \\ a & y & c \\ b & c & z\end{array}\right] \Rightarrow$$ Consider one such arrangement with $$a=\alpha, b=\beta, c=1$$

a, b, c can be arranged in 3! ways and corresponding entries can be arranged in 1 way.

(R)

$$\begin{aligned} & {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & a & b \\ -a & 0 & c \\ -b & -c & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} a \\ 0 \\ -c \end{array}\right]} \\ & a y+b z=a \\ & -a x+c z=0 \\ & -b x-c y=-c \end{aligned}$$

It is observed that $$\mathrm{D}=\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{z}}=0$$

$$\therefore$$ infinite solution

(S)

$$\begin{aligned} & {\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & \alpha & \beta \\ \beta & \alpha & 1 \\ \alpha & 1 & \beta \end{array}\right] } \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha \beta-1-\alpha \beta^2+\alpha^2+\beta^2-\alpha^2 \beta=0 \quad(\text { since } \alpha \beta=\alpha+\beta=-1) \end{aligned}$$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2024 Paper 1 Online
questions.question:
Let $\mathbb{R}^2$ denote $\mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{R}$. Let

$$ S=\left\{(a, b, c): a, b, c \in \mathbb{R} \text { and } a x^2+2 b x y+c y^2>0 \text { for all }(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2-\{(0,0)\}\right\} . $$

Then which of the following statements is (are) TRUE?
options:
For any given $(a, b, c) \in S$, the system of linear equations

$$ \begin{aligned} & a x+b y=1 \\ & b x+c y=-1 \end{aligned} $$

has a unique solution.

For any given $(a, b, c) \in S$, the system of linear equations

$$ \begin{aligned} & (a+1) x+b y=0 \\ & b x+(c+1) y=0 \end{aligned} $$

has a unique solution.
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{(A)}\quad & \mathrm{ax}^2+2 \mathrm{bxy}+\mathrm{cy}^2>0 \forall(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}) \in \mathbb{R}^2-\{(0,0)\} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{ax}+2 \mathrm{bxy}+\mathrm{cy}^2 \text { must represent pair of imaginary lines and } \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{c}>0 . \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{b}^2<\mathrm{ac} \end{aligned}$$

$$\mathrm{(B)}\quad\mathrm{b}^2<3 \times \frac{1}{12} \Rightarrow|2 \mathrm{~b}|<1$$

$$\mathrm{(C)}\quad$$ since $$\mathrm{b}^2 \neq \mathrm{ac}$$

$$\Rightarrow \mathrm{ax}+\mathrm{by}=1 \text { and } \mathrm{bx}+\mathrm{cy}=-1$$

are not parallel lines.

$$\mathrm{(D)}\quad\mathrm{ac}+\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c}>\mathrm{b}^2 \Rightarrow$$ lines are not parallel.

$$\Rightarrow$$ Options (B), (C), (D) are Correct.

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2024 Paper 1 Online
questions.question: Let $S=\left\{A=\left(\begin{array}{lll}0 & 1 & c \\ 1 & a & d \\ 1 & b & e\end{array}\right): a, b, c, d, e \in\{0,1\}\right.$ and $\left.|A| \in\{-1,1\}\right\}$, where $|A|$ denotes the determinant of $A$. Then the number of elements in $S$ is __________.
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} & |A|=0(a e-b d)-1(e-d)+c(b-a) \\ & =c(b-a)+(d-e) \end{aligned}$$

$$|\mathrm{A}| \in\{-1,1\}$$ and $$\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}, \mathrm{d}, \mathrm{e} \in\{0,1\}$$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Mathematics Delhi Set 1 - 2024
questions.question: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}a & c & -1 \\ b & 0 & 5 \\ 1 & -5 & 0\end{array}\right]$ is a skew-symmetric matrix, then the value of $2 a-(b+c)$ is
questions.solution:
We know that, if matrix A is skew symmetric, then

$$\begin{aligned} A^{\prime} & =-A \\ \therefore \quad\left[\begin{array}{ccc} a & b & 1 \\ c & 0 & -5 \\ -1 & 5 & 0 \end{array}\right] & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -a & -c & 1 \\ -b & 0 & -5 \\ -1 & 5 & 0 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

On comparing matrices, we get

$$\begin{aligned} a & =-a \Rightarrow 2 a=0 \Rightarrow a=0 \\ b & =-c \text { and } c=-b \end{aligned}$$

Now, $\quad 2 a+(b+c)=2 \times 0+(b-b)=0$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Mathematics Delhi Set 1 - 2024
questions.question: If $\left[\begin{array}{lll}x & 2 & 0\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c}5 \\ -1 \\ x\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll}3 & 1\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c}-2 \\ x\end{array}\right]$, then value of $x$ is
questions.solution:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} {\left[\begin{array}{lll} x & 2 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 5 \\ -1 \\ x \end{array}\right] } & =\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} -2 \\ x \end{array}\right] \\ 5 x-2 & =-6+x \\ 4 x & =-4 \\ x & =-1 \end{aligned}$$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Mathematics Delhi Set 1 - 2024
questions.question: Find the matrix $\mathrm{A}^2$, where $A=\left[a_{i j}\right]$ is a $2 \times 2$ matrix whose elements are given by $a_{i j}=$ maximum $(i, j)-$ minimum $(i, j)$
questions.solution:
Given for matrix $A=\left[\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ij}}\right]_{2 \times 2}$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \text { we have } & \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ij}}=\max (\mathrm{i}, \mathrm{j})-\min (\mathrm{i}, \mathrm{j}) \\ \therefore & a_{11}=1-1=0 \\ & a_{12}=2-1=1 \\ & a_{21}=2-1=1 \\ & a_{22}=2-2=0 \end{array}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ \text { Now, } & A^2=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \end{array}$$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Mathematics Delhi Set 1 - 2024
questions.question:
(a) If $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 0 & -3 \\ 1 & 2 & 0\end{array}\right]$, then find $A^{-1}$ and hence solve the following system of equations:

$$\begin{array}{r} x+2 y-3 z=1 \\ 2 x-3 z=2 \\ x+2 y=3 \end{array}$$

OR

(b) Find the product of the matrices $\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 3 & 2 \\ 3 & -3 & -4\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc}-6 & 17 & 13 \\ 14 & 5 & -8 \\ -15 & 9 & -1\end{array}\right]$ and hence solve the system of linear equations:

$$\begin{aligned} x+2 y-3 z & =4 \\ 2 x+3 y+2 z & =2 \\ 3 x-3 y-4 z & =11 \end{aligned}$$
questions.solution:
Given,

$$\begin{aligned} A & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 0 & -3 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ |A| & =1(0+6)-2(0+3)-3(4-0) \\ & =6-6-12=-12 \neq 0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow \text { inverse exists co-factors are }\\ &\begin{aligned} & C_{11}=(-1)^2\left|\begin{array}{cc} 0 & -3 \\ 2 & 0 \end{array}\right|=6 \\ & C_{12}=(-1)^3\left|\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -3 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right|=-3 \\ & C_{13}=(-1)^4\left|\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array}\right|=4 \\ & C_{21}=(-1)^3\left|\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 0 \end{array}\right|=-6 \\ & C_{22}=(-1)^4\left|\begin{array}{ll} 1 & -3 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right|=3 \\ & C_{23}=(-1)^5\left|\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & C_{31}=(-1)^4\left|\begin{array}{ll} 2 & -3 \\ 0 & -3 \end{array}\right|=-6 \\ & C_{32}=(-1)^5\left|\begin{array}{ll} 1 & -3 \\ 2 & -3 \end{array}\right|=-3 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &C_{33}=(-1)^6\left|\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 0 \end{array}\right|=-4\\ &\text { The adjoint of the matrix } \mathrm{A} \text { is given by }\\ &\begin{aligned} \operatorname{adj}(A) & =\left[\begin{array}{lll} C_{11} & C_{21} & C_{31} \\ C_{12} & C_{22} & C_{32} \\ C_{13} & C_{23} & C_{33} \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & -6 & -6 \\ -3 & 3 & -3 \\ 4 & 0 & -4 \end{array}\right] \\ \therefore \quad A^{-1} & =\frac{(\operatorname{adj} A)}{|A|}=-\frac{1}{12}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & -6 & -6 \\ -3 & 3 & -3 \\ 4 & 0 & -4 \end{array}\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{12}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -6 & 6 & 6 \\ 3 & -3 & 3 \\ -4 & 0 & 4 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

Given system of linear equations are

$$\begin{array}{r} x+2 y-3 z=1 \\ 2 x-3 z=2 \\ x+2 y=3 \end{array}$$

Represent it in matrix form

$$\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 0 & -3 \\ 0 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]$$

Which is in the form $A X=B$

$$X=A^{-1} B$$

$$\begin{aligned} &=\frac{1}{12}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -6 & 6 & 6 \\ 3 & -3 & 3 \\ -4 & 0 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right] \\ &=\frac{1}{12}\left[\begin{array}{c} 24 \\ 6 \\ 8 \end{array}\right] \\ &=\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ \frac{1}{2} \\ \frac{2}{3} \end{array}\right] \\ & \therefore \quad\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ \frac{1}{2} \\ \frac{2}{3} \end{array}\right] \\ & \text { or, } x=2, y=\frac{1}{2} \text { and } z=\frac{2}{3} \\ & \text { OR } \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { (b) We have, }\\ &\begin{aligned} A B & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 3 & 2 \\ 3 & -3 & -4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -6 & 17 & 13 \\ 14 & 5 & -8 \\ -15 & 9 & -1 \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -6+28+45 & 17+10-27 & 13-16+3 \\ -12+42-30 & 34+15+18 & 26-24-2 \\ -18-42+60 & 51-15-36 & 39+24+4 \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 67 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 67 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 67 \end{array}\right] \\ & =67\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} \text { Thus, } & A B =67 I \\ \Rightarrow & A\left(\frac{1}{67} B\right) =I \\ \Rightarrow & A^{-1} =\frac{1}{67}(B) \end{array}$$

Given system of linear equation is

$$\begin{aligned} x+2 y-3 z & =-4 \\ 2 x+3 y+2 z & =2 \\ 3 x-3 y-4 z & =11 \end{aligned}$$

Represent it in matrix form as

$$\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 3 & 2 \\ 3 & -3 & -4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} -4 \\ 2 \\ 11 \end{array}\right]$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { which is of the form } A X=D\\ &\therefore X=A^{-1} D\\ &\begin{aligned} &=\frac{1}{67}(B) \cdot D \\ &=\frac{1}{67}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -6 & 17 & 13 \\ 14 & 5 & -8 \\ -15 & 9 & -1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} -4 \\ 2 \\ 11 \end{array}\right] \\ &=\frac{1}{67}\left[\begin{array}{c} 201 \\ -134 \\ 67 \end{array}\right] \\ &=\left[\begin{array}{c} 3 \\ -2 \\ 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & \therefore x=3, y=-2 \text { and } z=1 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 1st September 2024
questions.question:
Let X be a matrix of order 3 x 3, Y be a matrix of order 2 x 3 and Z be a matrix of order 3 × 2. Which of the following statements are correct?

I. (ZY)X is defined and is a square matrix of order 3.

II. Y(XZ) is defined and is a square matrix of order 2.

III. X(YZ) is not defined.

Select the answer using the code given below.
questions.solution:
We will analyze the matrix operations:

For $ (ZY)X $:

$ Z $ is of order $ 3 \times 2 $ and $ Y $ is of order $ 2 \times 3 $, making $ ZY $ possible and resulting in a matrix of order $ 3 \times 3 $.

$ ZY $ is $ 3 \times 3 $ and $ X $ is $ 3 \times 3 $, making $ (ZY)X $ defined with a result that is a $ 3 \times 3 $ matrix.

For $ Y(XZ) $:

$ X $ is $ 3 \times 3 $ and $ Z $ is $ 3 \times 2 $, making $ XZ $ possible and resulting in a matrix of order $ 3 \times 2 $.

$ Y $ is $ 2 \times 3 $ and $ XZ $ becomes $ 3 \times 2 $, which results in $ Y(XZ) $ being defined and producing a matrix of order $ 2 \times 2 $.

For $ X(YZ) $:

$ Y $ is $ 2 \times 3 $ and $ Z $ is $ 3 \times 2 $, making $ YZ $ possible and resulting in a matrix of order $ 2 \times 2 $.

$ X $ is $ 3 \times 3 $ and cannot multiply with $ YZ $ as the number of columns in $ X $ (3) does not equal the number of rows in $ YZ $ (2), meaning $ X(YZ) $ is not defined.

Conclusion:

Statement I is correct: $ (ZY)X $ results in a square matrix of order $ 3 \times 3 $.

Statement II is correct: $ Y(XZ) $ results in a square matrix of order $ 2 \times 2 $.

Statement III is correct: $ X(YZ) $ is not defined because the column-row condition is not satisfied.

Therefore, all three statements are true.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 1st September 2024
questions.question: Let A and B be two square matrices of same order. If AB is a null matrix, then which one of the following is correct?
options: Both A and B are null matrices
Either A or B is a null matrix
B is a null matrix if A is a nonsingular matrix
Both A and B are singular matrices
questions.solution:
Given $ AB = O $, where $ A $ and $ B $ are square matrices of the same order, we want to determine the relationship between $ A $ and $ B $ based on this information.

Let's consider the matrices:

$ A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} $

$ B = \begin{bmatrix} p & q \\ r & s \end{bmatrix} $

The product $ AB $ is given by:

$ AB = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p & q \\ r & s \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} ap + br & aq + bs \\ cp + dr & cq + ds \end{bmatrix} $

Given $ AB = O $, the zero matrix:

$ \begin{bmatrix} ap + br & aq + bs \\ cp + dr & cq + ds \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $

From this, we compare and obtain the system of equations:

$ ap + br = 0 $

$ aq + bs = 0 $

$ cp + dr = 0 $

$ cq + ds = 0 $

From equations (1) and (3), we derive:

$(ad - bc)p = (ad - bc)r = 0$

From equations (2) and (4), we derive:

$(ad - bc)q = (ad - bc)s = 0$

Therefore, if $ A $ is non-singular, meaning $\det(A) = ad - bc \neq 0$, it implies that:

$ p = q = r = s = 0 $

Thus, matrix $ B $ must be the zero matrix if matrix $ A $ is non-singular.

Therefore, the statement "If $ A $ is non-singular, $ B $ is a null matrix" is correct.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 1st September 2024
questions.question:
Consider the following in respect of the matrices  $\rm P=\begin{bmatrix}0&c&-b\\\ -c&0&a\\\ b&-a&0\end{bmatrix}\ and\ \rm Q=\begin{bmatrix}a^2&ab&ac\\\ ab&b^2&bc\\\ ac&bc&c^2\end{bmatrix}$

I. PQ is a null matrix. 

II. QP is an identity matrix of order 3. 

III. PQ = QP

Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Given matrices are:

$ \text{P} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & c & -b \\ -c & 0 & a \\ b & -a & 0 \end{bmatrix} $

$ \text{Q} = \begin{bmatrix} a^2 & ab & ac \\ ab & b^2 & bc \\ ac & bc & c^2 \end{bmatrix} $

Calculate the product PQ:

$ \text{PQ} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $

Calculate the product QP:

$ \text{QP} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $

Since both PQ and QP yield null matrices, we have PQ = QP.

Conclusion: Both statements I and III are correct, indicating that the correct option is: "I and III".

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 1st September 2024
questions.question: What is A(adj A) equal to?
questions.solution:
We use the fact that for any invertible $A$,

$$A\,\mathrm{adj}(A)=\det(A)\,I.$$

Here

$ \det(A) =\begin{vmatrix} 3 & -3 & 4\\ 2 & -3 & 4\\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{vmatrix} =3\bigl((-3)(1)-4(-1)\bigr) -(-3)\bigl(2\cdot1-0\cdot4\bigr) +4\bigl(2(-1)-0(-3)\bigr) =3\cdot1+3\cdot2+4\cdot(-2) =1. $

Hence

$ A\,\mathrm{adj}(A)=1\cdot I =I =\begin{bmatrix} 1&0&0\\ 0&1&0\\ 0&0&1 \end{bmatrix}, $

so the correct choice is Option 4.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 1st September 2024
questions.question: What is A<sup>-1</sup> equal to?
questions.solution:
Let’s find $A^{-1}$ in three steps:

Compute $\det A$.

$ \det A = 3\det\!\begin{pmatrix}-3&4\\-1&1\end{pmatrix} -(-3)\det\!\begin{pmatrix}2&4\\0&1\end{pmatrix} +4\det\!\begin{pmatrix}2&-3\\0&-1\end{pmatrix} =3\,( -3+4)+3\,(2)+4\,(-2)=1. $

Build the cofactor matrix $C$ (entry $(i,j)$ is $(-1)^{i+j}$ times the minor):

$ C = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -2 & -2\\ -1 & 3 & 3\\ 0 & -4 & -3 \end{pmatrix}. $

Its transpose (the adjugate) is

$ \mathrm{adj}(A) = C^{T} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0\\ -2 & 3 & -4\\ -2 & 3 & -3 \end{pmatrix}. $

Divide by $\det A=1$. Thus

$ A^{-1} = \frac1{1}\,\mathrm{adj}(A) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0\\ -2 & 3 & -4\\ -2 & 3 & -3 \end{pmatrix}. $

That matches Option 1.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 21 April 2024
questions.question: Let $A$ and $B$ be matrices of order $3 \times 3$. If $|A| = \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}$ and $|B| = \frac{1}{729}$, then what is the value of $|2B(adj(3A))|$?
questions.solution:
To determine the value of $$|2B(\text{adj}(3A))|$$, we need to utilize some properties of determinants and adjugate matrices.

First, recall that for a square matrix $ A $ of order $ n $, the adjugate (or adjoint) of $ A $, denoted as $ \text{adj}(A) $, has a determinant related to the determinant of $ A $. Specifically, for a $ 3 \times 3 $ matrix, this relationship is:

$$ \text{adj}(A) = \text{det}(A) \cdot A^{-1} $$

Thus, the determinant of the adjugate of $ A $, for a $ 3 \times 3 $ matrix, is:

$$ \text{det}(\text{adj}(A)) = \text{det}(A)^{n-1} = \text{det}(A)^{2} $$

Given $ \text{det}(A) = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}} $, we can find:

$$ \text{det}(\text{adj}(3A)) = \left(\text{det}(3A)\right)^2 $$

Next, recall the determinant property for a scalar multiple of a matrix:

$$ \text{det}(kA) = k^n \cdot \text{det}(A) $$

For $ 3A $, we have:

$$ \text{det}(3A) = 3^3 \cdot \text{det}(A) = 27 \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{27}{2\sqrt{2}} $$

Thus:

$$ \text{det}(\text{adj}(3A)) = \left(\frac{27}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 = \frac{729}{8} $$

Now, for the matrix $ 2B $, use the scalar multiple property again:

$$ \text{det}(2B) = 2^3 \cdot \text{det}(B) = 8 \cdot \frac{1}{729} = \frac{8}{729} $$

We now combine these results to find:

$$ |2B (\text{adj}(3A))| = \text{det}(2B \cdot \text{adj}(3A)) = \text{det}(2B) \cdot \text{det}(\text{adj}(3A)) $$

Therefore:

$$ |2B (\text{adj}(3A))| = \frac{8}{729} \cdot \frac{729}{8} = 1 $$

So, the correct answer is:

D

1

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 21 April 2024
questions.question:
Consider the following statements in respect of two non-singular matrices $A$ and $B$ of the same order $n$:

  1. 1.$adj(AB) = (adjA)(adjB)$

  2. 2. $adj(AB) = adj(BA)$

  3. 3. $(AB) adj(AB) - |AB| I_n$ is a null matrix of order $n$

How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.solution:
To determine how many of the given statements are correct for two non-singular matrices $ A $ and $ B $ of the same order $ n $, let's analyze each statement individually.

Statement 1: $ \text{adj}(AB) = (\text{adj} A)(\text{adj} B) $

Explanation:

The adjugate of a product of two matrices is not equal to the product of their adjugates in the same order.

The correct property is:

$ \text{adj}(AB) = \text{adj}(B) \cdot \text{adj}(A) $

This is because the adjugate reverses the order when dealing with a product.

Conclusion: Statement 1 is incorrect.

Statement 2: $ \text{adj}(AB) = \text{adj}(BA) $

Explanation:

Using the property from Statement 1:

$ \text{adj}(AB) = \text{adj}(B) \cdot \text{adj}(A) $

$ \text{adj}(BA) = \text{adj}(A) \cdot \text{adj}(B) $

Unless $ \text{adj}(A) $ and $ \text{adj}(B) $ commute (which is not generally true), $ \text{adj}(AB) \neq \text{adj}(BA) $.

Therefore, without additional information about $ A $ and $ B $, we cannot conclude that $ \text{adj}(AB) = \text{adj}(BA) $.

Conclusion: Statement 2 is incorrect.

Statement 3: $ (AB) \cdot \text{adj}(AB) - |AB| I_n $ is a null matrix of order $ n $.

Explanation:

It is a fundamental property of matrices that for any square matrix $ M $:

$ M \cdot \text{adj}(M) = \det(M) \cdot I_n $

Applying this property to $ AB $:

$ (AB) \cdot \text{adj}(AB) = \det(AB) \cdot I_n $

Rearranging:

$ (AB) \cdot \text{adj}(AB) - \det(AB) \cdot I_n = 0 $

Which means the expression is indeed a null matrix.

Conclusion: Statement 3 is correct.

Final Answer:

Only Statement 3 is correct.

Answer:

$\boxed{\text{Only one statement}}$

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 21 April 2024
questions.question:
Consider the following statements in respect of a non-singular matrix $A$ of order $n$:

1. $A(\text{adj}A^T) = A(\text{adj}A)^T$

2. If $A^2 = A$, then $A$ is identity matrix of order $n$

3. If $A^3 = A$, then $A$ is identity matrix of order $n$

Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.solution:
To determine which statements are correct regarding a non-singular matrix $ A $ of order $ n $, let's analyze each statement individually.

Statement 1: $ A (\text{adj}\, A^T) = A (\text{adj}\, A)^T $

Explanation:

The adjugate of the transpose of $ A $ is equal to the transpose of the adjugate of $ A $:

$ \text{adj}\, (A^T) = (\text{adj}\, A)^T $

Therefore:

$ A (\text{adj}\, A^T) = A (\text{adj}\, A)^T $

Conclusion: Statement 1 is correct.

Statement 2: If $ A^2 = A $, then $ A $ is the identity matrix of order $ n $.

Explanation:

Given $ A^2 = A $, subtract $ A $ from both sides:

$ A^2 - A = 0 \implies A (A - I) = 0 $

Since $ A $ is non-singular (invertible), we can multiply both sides by $ A^{-1} $:

$ A^{-1} A (A - I) = A^{-1} 0 \implies (A - I) = 0 $

Therefore:

$ A = I $

Conclusion: Statement 2 is correct.

Statement 3: If $ A^3 = A $, then $ A $ is the identity matrix of order $ n $.

Explanation:

Given $ A^3 = A $, subtract $ A $ from both sides:

$ A^3 - A = 0 \implies A (A^2 - I) = 0 $

Since $ A $ is non-singular, multiply both sides by $ A^{-1} $:

$ A^{-1} A (A^2 - I) = A^{-1} 0 \implies (A^2 - I) = 0 $

So:

$ A^2 = I $

However, $ A^2 = I $ implies that $ A $ is a matrix such that its square is the identity. This means $ A $ could be $ I $ or $ -I $.

Since $ -I $ is also non-singular (its determinant is $ (-1)^n \neq 0 $), $ A $ does not have to be $ I $ necessarily.

Conclusion: Statement 3 is incorrect.

Final Answer:

Statements 1 and 2 are correct.

Statement 3 is incorrect.

Answer:

$\boxed{\text{1 and 2 only}}$

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 21 April 2024
questions.question:
Consider the following statements in respect of a skew-symmetric matrix $A$ of order $3$:

1. All diagonal elements are zero.

2. The sum of all the diagonal elements of the matrix is zero.

3. $A$ is orthogonal matrix.

Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.solution:
Let's analyze each statement given regarding a skew-symmetric matrix $ A $ of order 3:

1. All diagonal elements are zero.

For a skew-symmetric matrix $ A $, the property $ A^T = -A $ holds. For the diagonal elements, this implies $ a_{ii} = -a_{ii} $. The only solution to this equation is $ a_{ii} = 0 $. Therefore, all diagonal elements of a skew-symmetric matrix are indeed zero.

2. The sum of all the diagonal elements of the matrix is zero.

Since we have established that all diagonal elements $ a_{ii} $ are zero, summing up these zero elements will give us zero. Thus, the sum of all the diagonal elements for a skew-symmetric matrix is zero. This statement is correct.

3. $ A $ is an orthogonal matrix.

An orthogonal matrix $ Q $ satisfies $ Q^T Q = I $. However, a skew-symmetric matrix does not inherently have this property. While some special skew-symmetric matrices may also be orthogonal, it is not a general property of all skew-symmetric matrices. Therefore, a skew-symmetric matrix is not necessarily orthogonal.

Based on this analysis:

  • Statements 1 and 2 are correct.
  • Statement 3 is not necessarily correct for all skew-symmetric matrices.

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option A: 1 and 2 only

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 21 April 2024
questions.question:
If $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}\sin 2 \theta & 2 \sin ^2 \theta-1 & 0 \\ \cos 2 \theta & 2 \sin \theta \cos \theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right]$, then which of the following statements is/are correct?

1. $A^{-1}=\operatorname{adj} A$

2. A is skew-symmetric matrix

3. $A^{-1}=A^T$

Select the correct answer using the code given below :
questions.solution:
Let's analyze each statement and determine their validity:

Statement 1: $A^{-1} = \text{adj} A$

For any square matrix A, the following relationship holds:

$$A^{-1} = \frac{1}{|A|} \text{adj} A$$

where |A| is the determinant of A, and adj A is the adjugate of A.

To prove statement 1, we need to show that |A| = 1. Let's calculate the determinant of A:

$$|A| = \begin{vmatrix} \sin 2\theta & 2\sin^2 \theta - 1 & 0 \\ \cos 2\theta & 2\sin\theta\cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix} $$

Expanding along the third row, we get:

$$|A| = 1 \begin{vmatrix} \sin 2\theta & 2\sin^2 \theta - 1 \\ \cos 2\theta & 2\sin\theta\cos\theta \end{vmatrix} = \sin 2\theta (2\sin\theta\cos\theta) - (2\sin^2 \theta - 1)(\cos 2\theta)$$

Simplifying using the double-angle formulas ($sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$ and $\cos 2\theta = 1 - 2\sin^2 \theta$):

$$|A| = 2\sin^2 \theta \cos^2 \theta - (2\sin^2 \theta - 1)(1 - 2\sin^2 \theta) = 1$$

Since |A| = 1, we can conclude that $A^{-1} = \text{adj} A$. Therefore, statement 1 is correct.

Statement 2: A is a skew-symmetric matrix

A skew-symmetric matrix is a square matrix where the transpose is equal to the negative of the original matrix, i.e., $A^T = -A$.

Let's find the transpose of A:

$$A^T = \begin{bmatrix} \sin 2\theta & \cos 2\theta & 0 \\ 2\sin^2 \theta - 1 & 2\sin\theta\cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Comparing $A^T$ and $-A$, we can see that they are not equal. Therefore, statement 2 is incorrect.

Statement 3: $A^{-1} = A^T$

We already know that $A^{-1} = \text{adj} A$. To prove statement 3, we need to show that $\text{adj} A = A^T$.

Let's calculate the adjugate of A. The adjugate is the transpose of the cofactor matrix:

First, find the cofactor matrix of A:

$$C = \begin{bmatrix} \begin{vmatrix} 2\sin\theta\cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix} & -\begin{vmatrix} \cos 2\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix} & \begin{vmatrix} \cos 2\theta & 2\sin\theta\cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 \end{vmatrix} \\ -\begin{vmatrix} 2\sin^2 \theta - 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix} & \begin{vmatrix} \sin 2\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix} & -\begin{vmatrix} \sin 2\theta & 2\sin^2 \theta - 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{vmatrix} \\ \begin{vmatrix} 2\sin^2 \theta - 1 & 0 \\ 2\sin\theta\cos\theta & 0 \end{vmatrix} & -\begin{vmatrix} \sin 2\theta & 0 \\ \cos 2\theta & 0 \end{vmatrix} & \begin{vmatrix} \sin 2\theta & 2\sin^2 \theta - 1 \\ \cos 2\theta & 2\sin\theta\cos\theta \end{vmatrix} \end{bmatrix}$$

Simplifying the determinants, we get:

$$C = \begin{bmatrix} 2\sin\theta\cos\theta & -\cos 2\theta & 0 \\ -(2\sin^2 \theta - 1) & \sin 2\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Now, find the transpose of the cofactor matrix:

$$ \text{adj} A = C^T = \begin{bmatrix} 2\sin\theta\cos\theta & -(2\sin^2 \theta - 1) & 0 \\ -\cos 2\theta & \sin 2\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Comparing $\text{adj} A$ with $A^T$, we find they are equal. Therefore, statement 3 is correct.

Conclusion:

The correct statements are 1 and 3. Therefore, the answer is Option C: 1 and 3.

questions.title: BITSAT 2024
questions.question: A projectile is projected with velocity of $ 40 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $ at an angle $ \theta $ with the horizontal. If $ R $ be the horizontal range covered by the projectile and after $ t $ seconds, its inclination with horizontal becomes zero, then the value of $ \cot \theta $ is [Take, $ g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2} $ ]
questions.solution:
When a projectile is launched with a velocity of $ 40 \, \text{m/s} $ at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal, and its inclination with the horizontal becomes zero after $ t $ seconds, we want to determine the value of $\cot \theta$.

Maximum Height Condition: The projectile's inclination with the horizontal becomes zero at its maximum height.

Time to Reach Maximum Height:

$ t = \frac{u \sin \theta}{g} $

Solving for $ u $, we get:

$ u = \frac{g t}{\sin \theta} \quad \ldots \text{(i)} $

Horizontal Range $ R $:

$ R = u \cos \theta \times (2t) $

Solving for $\cos \theta$:

$ \cos \theta = \frac{R}{2u t} $

Substituting $ u $ from (i):

$ \cos \theta = \frac{R}{2 \cdot \frac{g t}{\sin \theta} \cdot t} $

Simplifying gives:

$ \cos \theta = \frac{R \sin \theta}{2 g t^2} $

Finding $\cot \theta$:

$ \cot \theta = \frac{\cos \theta}{\sin \theta} = \frac{R}{2 g t^2} $

Substituting $ g = 10 \, \text{m/s}^2 $:

$ \cot \theta = \frac{R}{2 \times 10 t^2} = \frac{R}{20 t^2} $

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2024 Paper 2 Online
questions.question: A ball is thrown from the location $\left(x_0, y_0\right)=(0,0)$ of a horizontal playground with an initial speed $v_0$ at an angle $\theta_0$ from the $+x$-direction. The ball is to be hit by a stone, which is thrown at the same time from the location $\left(x_1, y_1\right)=(L, 0)$. The stone is thrown at an angle $\left(180-\theta_1\right)$ from the $+x$-direction with a suitable initial speed. For a fixed $v_0$, when $\left(\theta_0, \theta_1\right)=\left(45^{\circ}, 45^{\circ}\right)$, the stone hits the ball after time $T_1$, and when $\left(\theta_0, \theta_1\right)=\left(60^{\circ}, 30^{\circ}\right)$, it hits the ball after time $T_2$. In such a case, $\left(T_1 / T_2\right)^2$ is ______.
questions.solution:
For Case I :

$$\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{rel}}=0$$

For collision $$\frac{\mathrm{v}_0}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\sqrt{2}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \mathrm{v}=\mathrm{v}_0 \\ & \text { So } \mathrm{T}_1=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\frac{\mathrm{v}_0}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\sqrt{2}}} \\ & \therefore \tau_1=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\sqrt{2} \mathrm{v}_0} \quad \text{... (1)} \end{aligned}$$

For case II,

$$\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{rel}}=0$$

For collision, $$\frac{v_0 \sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{v}{2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \mathrm{v}=\sqrt{3} \mathrm{v}_0 \\ & \text { So, } \mathrm{T}_2=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\frac{\mathrm{v}_0}{2}+\mathrm{v} \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}} \\ & \mathrm{~T}_2=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\frac{\mathrm{v}_0}{2}+\frac{3 \mathrm{v}_0}{2}} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{T}_2=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2 \mathrm{v}_0} \quad \ldots . .(2) \\ & \text { so, }\left(\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}\right)^2=(\sqrt{2})^2=2 \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}\right)^2=2 \end{aligned}$$

questions.title: BITSAT 2024
questions.question: The straight wire $ A B $ carries a current $ I $. The ends of the wire subtend angles $ \theta_{1} $ and $ \theta_{2} $ at the point $ P $ as shown in figure. The magnetic field at the point $ P $ is
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at a point $ P $ due to a current carrying straight wire, such that the ends of the wires, makes angle $ \alpha $ and $ \beta $ (see figure) is given by

$ B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi d}(\sin \alpha-\sin \beta) $

where, $ d $ is the distance between the point $ P $ and wire.

In the given problem, $ \alpha=\theta_{1} $ and $ \beta=\theta_{2} $

$ \therefore \quad B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi d}\left(\sin \theta_{1}-\sin \theta_{2}\right) $

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2024 Paper 1 Online
questions.question: At room temperature, disproportionation of an aqueous solution of in situ generated nitrous acid $\left(\mathrm{HNO}_2\right)$ gives the species
questions.solution:
To determine the products of the disproportionation of nitrous acid ($$ \mathrm{HNO}_2 $$) at room temperature, let's analyze the chemical behavior of $$ \mathrm{HNO}_2 $$ in aqueous solution.

Disproportionation is a redox reaction in which a single substance is simultaneously oxidized and reduced, yielding two different products. In the case of nitrous acid ($$ \mathrm{HNO}_2 $$), the reaction can be represented as follows:

$$ 3 \mathrm{HNO}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{HNO}_3 + \mathrm{H_2O} + 2 \mathrm{NO} $$

Breaking this down, we see that nitrous acid disproportionates into nitric acid ($$ \mathrm{HNO}_3 $$), water ($$ \mathrm{H_2O} $$), and nitrogen monoxide ($$ \mathrm{NO} $$). Additionally, in an aqueous solution, nitric acid dissociates to form $$ \mathrm{H_3O}^+ $$ (hydronium ion) and $$ \mathrm{NO_3}^- $$ (nitrate ion).

Therefore, the species formed from this disproportionation reaction are $$ \mathrm{H_3O}^+ $$, $$ \mathrm{NO_3}^- $$, and $$ \mathrm{NO} $$.

From the given options, the correct answer is:

Option A

$$ \mathrm{H_3O}^+ $$, $$ \mathrm{NO_3}^- $$, and $$ \mathrm{NO} $$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2024 Paper 2 Online
questions.question: The species formed on fluorination of phosphorus pentachloride in a polar organic solvent are
questions.solution:
When $ \text{PCl}_5 $ is fluorinated in a polar organic solvent, it forms ionic isomers. The formation of these ionic species can be understood as follows:

  1. $ \text{PCl}_5 $ in a polar organic solvent can dissociate into ionic forms, such as $ \left[\text{PCl}_4\right]^+ $ and $ \left[\text{PCl}_6\right]^- $.

  2. Upon fluorination, $ \text{PCl}_5 $ can be converted into $ \left[\text{PF}_6\right]^- $ and $ \left[\text{PCl}_4\right]^+ $.

  3. Further fluorination can lead to the formation of mixed ionic compounds where $ \text{PCl}_5 $ can form isomers like $ \left[\text{PCl}_4\right]^+ \left[\text{PCl}_4 \text{F}_2\right]^- $.

The two ionic isomers formed are:

  1. $ \left[\text{PCl}_4\right]^+ \left[\text{PCl}_4 \text{F}_2\right]^- $, which are colorless crystals.

  2. $ \left[\text{PCl}_4\right]^+ \left[\text{PF}_6\right]^- $, which are white crystals.

Hence, the ionic isomers formed when $ \text{PCl}_5 $ is fluorinated in a polar organic solvent are :

Option B : $ \left[\text{PCl}_4\right]^+ \left[\text{PCl}_4 \text{F}_2\right]^- $ and $ \left[\text{PCl}_4\right]^+ \left[\text{PF}_6\right]^- $

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2024 Paper 2 Online
questions.question: Let $A_1, B_1, C_1$ be three points in the $x y$-plane. Suppose that the lines $A_1 C_1$ and $B_1 C_1$ are tangents to the curve $y^2=8 x$ at $A_1$ and $B_1$, respectively. If $O=(0,0)$ and $C_1=(-4,0)$, then which of the following statements is (are) TRUE?
options: The length of the line segment $O A_1$ is $4 \sqrt{3}$
The length of the line segment $A_1 B_1$ is 16
The orthocenter of the triangle $A_1 B_1 C_1$ is $(0,0)$
The orthocenter of the triangle $A_1 B_1 C_1$ is $(1,0)$
questions.solution:
Equation of tangent at (2t$$^2$$, 4t) is

$$\mathrm{ty}=\mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{t}^2$$

$$\because$$ It is passing through $$(-4,0)$$

$$\therefore 0=-4+2 \mathrm{t}^2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}= \pm \sqrt{2}$$

$$\left.\begin{array}{rl} \therefore & \mathrm{A}_1=(4,4 \sqrt{2}) \\ & \mathrm{B}_1=(4,-4 \sqrt{2}) \end{array}\right\} \quad \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{OA}_1=\sqrt{48}=4 \sqrt{3} \\ & \mathrm{~A}_1 \mathrm{~B}_1=8 \sqrt{2} \end{aligned}$$

Equation of altitude of $$\Delta A_1 B_1 C_1$$ drawn from $$A_1$$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & y-4 \sqrt{2}=\sqrt{2}(x-4) \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{2} x-y=0 \quad \text{... (1)} \end{aligned}$$

Equation of altitude of $$\Delta A_1 B_1 C_1$$ drawn from $$C_1$$ is

$$\mathrm{x}=0\quad \text{... (2)}$$

Solving (1) and (2) $$\Rightarrow$$ orthocentre is $$(0,0)$$

$$\therefore$$ correct options are (A), (C)

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2024 Paper 2 Online
questions.question: A normal with slope $\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}$ is drawn from the point $(0,-\alpha)$ to the parabola $x^2=-4 a y$, where $a>0$. Let $L$ be the line passing through $(0,-\alpha)$ and parallel to the directrix of the parabola. Suppose that $L$ intersects the parabola at two points $A$ and $B$. Let $r$ denote the length of the latus rectum and $s$ denote the square of the length of the line segment $A B$. If $r: s=1: 16$, then the value of $24 a$ is _______.
questions.solution:
$$\frac{d y}{d x}=\left.\frac{x}{-2 a} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}\right|_N=-t$$

Slope of normal $$=\frac{1}{t}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \Rightarrow t=\sqrt{6}$$

Now, $$\frac{-\mathrm{at}^2+\alpha}{2 \mathrm{at}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}}$$

$$\Rightarrow-\mathrm{at}^2+\alpha=2 \mathrm{a}$$

$$\Rightarrow-6 \mathrm{a}+\alpha=2 \mathrm{a} \Rightarrow \alpha=8 \mathrm{a}$$

For A and B

$$\begin{aligned} & x^2=-4 a(-8 a) \\ & \Rightarrow x^2=32 a^2 \Rightarrow x= \pm 4 \sqrt{2} a \\ & \therefore A(-4 \sqrt{2} a,-8 a), B(4 \sqrt{2} a,-8 a) \\ & \therefore A B^2=(8 \sqrt{2} a)^2=128 a^2=s \\ & \therefore \text { Length of } L R=r=4 a \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{r}{s}=\frac{4 a}{128 a^2}=\frac{1}{16} \\ & \therefore 32 a=16 \Rightarrow a=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \therefore 24 a=12 \text { Ans. } \end{aligned}$$

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 1st September 2024
questions.question: Consider the points P(4k, 4k) and Q(4k, -4k) lying on the parabola y<sup>2</sup> = 4kx. If the vertex is A, then what is ∠PAQ equal to?
questions.solution:
To find the angle ∠PAQ, we start by considering the points P(4k, 4k) and Q(4k, -4k) on the parabola given by the equation $ y^2 = 4kx $.

Step-by-Step Explanation:

Identify and Define Angles:

Let ∠PAM be denoted by $ \alpha $.

Let ∠APM be denoted by $ \beta $.

Use Geometry:

It’s clear from the figure that ∠PAQ is made up of two angles:

$ ∠PAQ = ∠PAM + ∠QAM $

Since $\alpha = \beta$, we have:

$ ∠PAQ = \alpha + \alpha = 2\alpha $

Form a Right Triangle:

In the right-angled triangle AMP, we note:

AM = PM = 4k (which are the distances from a common point).

This implies that $\alpha = \beta$.

Calculate the Angles:

Since $\alpha + \beta + 90° = 180°$ (the sum of angles in a triangle),

$ \alpha + \alpha + 90° = 180° $

Solving gives:

$ 2\alpha = 90° $

$ \alpha = 45° $

Find ∠PAQ:

Substituting back:

$ ∠PAQ = 2\alpha = 90° $

Therefore, ∠PAQ is 90 degrees.

Conclusion:

∴ The correct angle ∠PAQ is 90°, confirming the answer.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 21 April 2024
questions.question:
In the parabola $y^2 = 8x$, the focal distance of a point P lying on it is 8 units. Which of the following statements is/are correct?

1. The coordinates of $P$ can be $\left(6, 4\sqrt{3}\right)$.

2. The perpendicular distance of $P$ from the directrix of parabola is 8 units.

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.solution:
To determine which statements about the point $ P $ lying on the parabola $ y^2 = 8x $ are correct, let us analyze each statement in detail.

Step 1: Understanding the Focal Distance and Directrix of the Parabola

The given equation of the parabola is $ y^2 = 8x $. The standard form of a parabola that opens to the right is $ y^2 = 4ax $. Comparing this with the given equation, we get $ 4a = 8 $, which gives $ a = 2 $.

Here, $ a $ is the distance from the vertex to the focus of the parabola. Therefore, the focus of the given parabola is at $ (2, 0) $, and the directrix is the vertical line $ x = -2 $.

Step 2: Checking Statement 1

Given that the coordinates of $ P $ can be $ (6, 4\sqrt{3}) $, we need to confirm whether this point lies on the parabola $ y^2 = 8x $.

Substitute $ y = 4\sqrt{3} $ and $ x = 6 $ into $ y^2 = 8x $:

$ (4\sqrt{3})^2 = 8 \cdot 6 \implies 48 = 48 $

Since this equation holds true, $ P = (6, 4\sqrt{3}) $ lies on the parabola.

We are also given that the focal distance (distance from point $ P $ to the focus) is 8 units. Calculate the distance from $ P = (6, 4\sqrt{3}) $ to the focus $ (2, 0) $:

$ \text{Distance} = \sqrt{(6 - 2)^2 + (4\sqrt{3} - 0)^2} = \sqrt{4^2 + (4\sqrt{3})^2} = \sqrt{16 + 48} = \sqrt{64} = 8 $

Therefore, Statement 1 is correct.

Step 3: Checking Statement 2

We need to determine the perpendicular distance from $ P = (6, 4\sqrt{3}) $ to the directrix $ x = -2 $, which is given as 8 units.

The perpendicular distance from a point $ (x_1, y_1) $ to the line $ x = -2 $ is given by $ |x_1 - (-2)| = |x_1 + 2| $.

For $ P = (6, 4\sqrt{3}) $, the perpendicular distance to the directrix is:

$ |6 + 2| = |8| = 8 $

This indeed is 8 units. Therefore, Statement 2 is also correct.

Conclusion

Both statements are correct. Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option C

Both 1 and 2

questions.title: BITSAT 2024
questions.question: The ionic radii in $ \mathring{A} $ of $ \mathrm{N}^{3-}, \mathrm{O}^{2-} $ and $ \mathrm{F}^{-} $are respectively.
questions.solution:
$ \mathrm{N}^{3-}, \mathrm{O}^{2-} $ and $ \mathrm{F}^{-} $are isoelectronics ( 10 electrons) species. Their ionic radii will decrease with increase in magnitude of the nuclear charge from $ \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{Z}=7) $ to O $ (Z=8) $ and $ F(Z=9) $.

i.e.

$ \begin{array}{c} \mathrm{N}^{3-} > \mathrm{O}^{2-} > \mathrm{F}^{-} \\ 1.71 \mathring{A} \quad 1.40 \mathring{A} \quad 1.36 \mathring{A} \end{array} $

Hence, option (a) is correct.

questions.title: BITSAT 2024
questions.question: Electron affinity is positive, when
questions.solution:
When $ \mathrm{O}^{-} $changes into $ \mathrm{O}^{2-} $, energy is absorbed i.e the change is endothermic because $ \mathrm{O}^{-} $repels the incoming electron due to similar charge.

Hence, it needs energy to add (accept) the electron.

So, electron affinity $ \left(\mathrm{EA}_{2}\right) $ is positive.

questions.title: BITSAT 2024
questions.question: How many different nine digit numbers can be formed from the number 223355888 by rearranging its digits so that the odd digits occupy even positions
questions.solution:
To solve the problem, we need to arrange the digits of the number 223355888 so that the odd digits occupy even positions. We have:

Odd digits: $3, 3, 5, 5$

Even digits: $2, 2, 8, 8, 8$

The number positions can be represented as:

$ \bar{O} \bar{E} \bar{O} \bar{E} \bar{O} \bar{E} \bar{O} \bar{E} \bar{O} $

Where $E$ indicates even positions and $O$ indicates odd positions.

Steps:

Arranging Odd Digits on Even Positions:

There are 4 even positions available for the odd digits $ (3, 3, 5, 5) $. The number of ways to arrange these with repetitions is given by:

$ \frac{4!}{2!2!} $

Arranging Even Digits on Odd Positions:

There are 5 odd positions available for the even digits $ (2, 2, 8, 8, 8) $. The number of ways to arrange these with repetitions is given by:

$ \frac{5!}{2!3!} $

Calculating Total Arrangements:

By multiplying the number of ways to arrange the odd and even digits, we get the total number of 9-digit numbers that can be formed:

$ \frac{4!}{2!2!} \times \frac{5!}{2!3!} = 60 $

Thus, there are $60$ different ways to arrange the digits to meet the given conditions.

questions.title: BITSAT 2024
questions.question: A person invites a party of 10 friends at dinner and place so that 4 are on one round table and 6 on the other round table. The number of ways in which he can arrange the guests is
questions.solution:
To find the number of ways to arrange 10 friends at dinner on two round tables, one with 4 people and the other with 6, follow these steps:

Divide into Groups:

You need to create two groups from 10 people: one group with 4 people and another with 6 people.

The number of ways to form these groups is given by the combination formula:

$ \frac{10!}{4! \times 6!} $

Arrange on Round Tables:

Arranging 4 people on a round table can be done in $(4-1)!$ ways (since arrangements around a circle account for rotations being identical):

$ 3! $

Arranging 6 people on a round table can be done in $(6-1)!$ ways:

$ 5! $

Total Arrangements:

Multiply the number of ways to form the groups by the number of ways to arrange each group on their respective tables:

$ \frac{10!}{4! \times 6!} \times 3! \times 5! $

Simplifying, this expression calculates to:

$ \frac{10!}{24} $

Hence, the total number of ways to arrange the guests is $\frac{10!}{24}$.

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2024 Paper 1 Online

Suppose that $s_1$ cannot be selected for the team $X$, and $s_2$ cannot be selected for the team $Y$. Then the number of ways to form such teams, is ____________.
